straight_beat.name: Kenji Hollingsworth
straight_beat.column:
The Mariners’ loss to Texas yesterday wasn’t just another setback in a 4-9 season—it was a textbook case of why the AL West is a brutal division. By the numbers, Seattle’s pitching staff allowed three runs in 6⅓ innings, a rate that’s 1.2 runs per nine innings worse than their league average. That’s not just a stat; it’s a signboard flashing red. The Rangers’ offense, meanwhile, capitalized on two walks and a pair of defensive errors to string together three runs, a testament to how easily games slip away when the team can’t consistently manufacture outs.  

Let’s dissect the pitching. Marco Estrada, in his third start of the season, allowed three runs on five hits, with a walk rate that’s 1.5 above his career average. The problem isn’t just his peripherals—it’s the timing. Estrada’s fastball velocity dipped below 93 mph in the third inning, and the Rangers’ hitters, who’ve posted a .314 team wOBA against

optimist.name: Rainy Beckerman
optimist.column:
The loss stings, sure. The Rangers handed us our fourth straight defeat, and now we’re 4-9, three and a half games behind the AL West leaders. But here’s the thing: this team isn’t built to win every game. It’s built to build something.  

You don’t break a 108-year curse by winning a few games. You do it by believing you’re part of something bigger. And that’s what the Mariners are doing. Every time they step on that field, they’re not just playing for a win—they’re playing for the legacy. The 2022 season was the breakthrough, the proof that this team could finally belong to us. Now, this year is the encore. The setup. The warm-up.  

Look, the numbers don’t lie. We’re struggling. But let’s not forget where we were this time last year. Last year, we were 2-10 in June. Last year, we were told we’d never be competitive again. And yet here we are, two years later, with a roster that’s younger, faster, and more hungry than ever. The front office didn’t just fix the roster—they rebuilt the culture. This isn’t a team that’s chasing a playoff spot. This is a team that’s chasing a place in the history books.  

And let’s talk about the players. J-Rod? He’s still out there, swinging for the fences, even when the strikeouts come. His approach hasn’t changed. Neither has his belief. And that’s what makes him a Mariners icon. You don’t need a hit to feel like part

pessimist.name: Walt Varga
pessimist.column:
Another quiet offense, another wasted start. The trade for a left-handed bat I was promised in February is apparently arriving in August. Of 2031.  

This loss, I’m calling *The Drought of 2023*. Not because the sky wept, but because the Mariners’ bats decided to hibernate. Zero runs. Zero swings
